question:
4. If \( a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots, a_{n} \) are complex numbers such that
\[
\left|a_{1}\right|=\left|a_{2}\right|=\cdots=\left|a_{n}\right|=r \neq 0
\]
and if \( { }_{n} T_{s} \) denotes the sum of all products of these \( n \) numbers taken \( s \) at a time, prove that
\[
\left|\frac{{ }_{n} T_{s}}{{ }_{n} T_{n-s}}\right|=r^{2 s-n}
\]
whenever the denominator of the left-hand side is different from zero.
solution:
Solution. For any non-zero complex number, \( z \), we have \( z \quad 1=\bar{z} /|z|^{2} \) where the bar denotes complex conjugation. If \( J \) is a set of \( s \) indices selected from \( \{1,2, \ldots, n\} \), then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\prod_{i \pounds  J} a_{i} & =a_{1} a_{2} \cdots a_{n} \prod_{i \in J} a_{i}^{-1}=a_{1} a_{2} \cdots a_{n} \prod_{i \in J} \frac{\bar{a}_{i}}{r^{2}} \\
& =\frac{a_{1} a_{2} \cdots a_{n}}{r^{2 s}} \prod_{i \in J} \bar{a}_{i}
\end{aligned}
\]

Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
{ }_{n} T_{n-s} & =\sum_{J} \prod_{i \notin J} a_{i}=\frac{a_{1} a_{2} \cdots a_{n}}{r^{2 s}} \sum_{J} \prod_{i \in J} \overline{a_{1}} \\
& =\frac{a_{1} a_{2} \cdots a_{n}}{r^{2 s}} \bar{T}_{s} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence
\[
\left.\left|{ }_{n} T_{n-s}\right|=\left.\frac{r^{n}}{r^{2 s}}\right|_{n} \overline{T_{s}}\left|=r^{n-2 s}\right|_{n} T_{s} \right\rvert\,,
\]
whence the required formula follows at once.

descriptive_long.question:
4. If \( alphaone, alphatwo, \ldots, alphanlast \) are complex numbers such that
\[
\left|alphaone\right|=\left|alphatwo\right|=\cdots=\left|alphanlast\right|=modulus \neq 0
\]
and if \( { }_{totalnum} T_{subsetsize} \) denotes the sum of all products of these \( totalnum \) numbers taken \( subsetsize \) at a time, prove that
\[
\left|\frac{{ }_{totalnum} T_{subsetsize}}{{ }_{totalnum} T_{totalnum-subsetsize}}\right|=modulus^{2 subsetsize-totalnum}
\]
whenever the denominator of the left-hand side is different from zero.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. For any non-zero complex number, \( complexz \), we have \( complexz \quad 1=\bar{complexz} /|complexz|^{2} \) where the bar denotes complex conjugation. If \( indexset \) is a set of \( subsetsize \) indices selected from \( \{1,2, \ldots, totalnum\} \), then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\prod_{indexer \in indexset} alphavar & =alphaone alphatwo \cdots alphanlast \prod_{indexer \in indexset} alphavar^{-1}=alphaone alphatwo \cdots alphanlast \prod_{indexer \in indexset} \frac{\bar{alphavar}}{modulus^{2}} \\
& =\frac{alphaone alphatwo \cdots alphanlast}{modulus^{2 subsetsize}} \prod_{indexer \in indexset} \bar{alphavar}
\end{aligned}
\]

Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
{ }_{totalnum} T_{totalnum-subsetsize} & =\sum_{indexset} \prod_{indexer \notin indexset} alphavar=\frac{alphaone alphatwo \cdots alphanlast}{modulus^{2 subsetsize}} \sum_{indexset} \prod_{indexer \in indexset} \overline{alphavar} \\
& =\frac{alphaone alphatwo \cdots alphanlast}{modulus^{2 subsetsize}} \bar{T}_{subsetsize} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence
\[
\left.\left|{ }_{totalnum} T_{totalnum-subsetsize}\right|=\left.\frac{modulus^{totalnum}}{modulus^{2 subsetsize}}\right|_{totalnum} \overline{T_{subsetsize}}\left|=modulus^{totalnum-2 subsetsize}\right|_{totalnum} T_{subsetsize} \right\rvert\,,
\]
whence the required formula follows at once.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
4. If \( pineapple, toothbrush, \ldots, raincloud \) are complex numbers such that
\[
\left|pineapple\right|=\left|toothbrush\right|=\cdots=\left|raincloud\right|=pendulum \neq 0
\]
and if \( { }_{tortoise} T_{goldfish} \) denotes the sum of all products of these \( tortoise \) numbers taken \( goldfish \) at a time, prove that
\[
\left|\frac{{ }_{tortoise} T_{goldfish}}{{ }_{tortoise} T_{tortoise-goldfish}}\right|=pendulum^{2 goldfish-tortoise}
\]
whenever the denominator of the left-hand side is different from zero.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. For any non-zero complex number, \( buttercup \), we have \( buttercup \quad 1=\bar{buttercup} /|buttercup|^{2} \) where the bar denotes complex conjugation. If \( scarecrow \) is a set of \( goldfish \) indices selected from \( \{1,2, \ldots, tortoise\} \), then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\prod_{lanternfly \pounds  scarecrow} snowflake & =pineapple toothbrush \cdots raincloud \prod_{lanternfly \in scarecrow} snowflake^{-1}=pineapple toothbrush \cdots raincloud \prod_{lanternfly \in scarecrow} \frac{\bar{snowflake}}{pendulum^{2}} \\
& =\frac{pineapple toothbrush \cdots raincloud}{pendulum^{2 goldfish}} \prod_{lanternfly \in scarecrow} \bar{snowflake}
\end{aligned}
\]

Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
{ }_{tortoise} T_{tortoise-goldfish} & =\sum_{scarecrow} \prod_{lanternfly \notin scarecrow} snowflake=\frac{pineapple toothbrush \cdots raincloud}{pendulum^{2 goldfish}} \sum_{scarecrow} \prod_{lanternfly \in scarecrow} \overline{pineapple} \\
& =\frac{pineapple toothbrush \cdots raincloud}{pendulum^{2 goldfish}} \bar{T}_{goldfish} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence
\[
\left.\left|{ }_{tortoise} T_{tortoise-goldfish}\right|=\left.\frac{pendulum^{tortoise}}{pendulum^{2 goldfish}}\right|_{tortoise} \overline{T_{goldfish}}\left|=pendulum^{tortoise-2 goldfish}\right|_{tortoise} T_{goldfish} \right\rvert\,,
\]
whence the required formula follows at once.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
4. If \( finaldatum, ultimatepiece, \ldots, firstdatum \) are complex numbers such that
\[
\left|finaldatum\right|=\left|ultimatepiece\right|=\cdots=\left|firstdatum\right|=flatness \neq 0
\]
and if \( { }_{emptiness} T_{entirety} \) denotes the sum of all products of these \( emptiness \) numbers taken \( entirety \) at a time, prove that
\[
\left|\frac{{ }_{emptiness} T_{entirety}}{{ }_{emptiness} T_{emptiness-entirety}}\right|=flatness^{2 entirety-emptiness}
\]
whenever the denominator of the left-hand side is different from zero.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. For any non-zero complex number, \( realzero \), we have \( realzero \quad 1=\bar{realzero} /|realzero|^{2} \) where the bar denotes complex conjugation. If \( singularset \) is a set of \( entirety \) indices selected from \{1,2, \ldots, emptiness\}, then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\prod_{constantidx \pounds  singularset} staticunit & =finaldatum ultimatepiece \cdots firstdatum \prod_{constantidx \in singularset} staticunit^{-1}=finaldatum ultimatepiece \cdots firstdatum \prod_{constantidx \in singularset} \frac{\bar{staticunit}}{flatness^{2}} \\
& =\frac{finaldatum ultimatepiece \cdots firstdatum}{flatness^{2 entirety}} \prod_{constantidx \in singularset} \bar{staticunit}
\end{aligned}
\]

Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
{ }_{emptiness} T_{emptiness-entirety} & =\sum_{singularset} \prod_{constantidx \notin singularset} staticunit=\frac{finaldatum ultimatepiece \cdots firstdatum}{flatness^{2 entirety}} \sum_{singularset} \prod_{constantidx \in singularset} \overline{finaldatum} \\
& =\frac{finaldatum ultimatepiece \cdots firstdatum}{flatness^{2 entirety}} \bar{T}_{entirety} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence
\[
\left.\left|{ }_{emptiness} T_{emptiness-entirety}\right|=\left.\frac{flatness^{emptiness}}{flatness^{2 entirety}}\right|_{emptiness} \overline{T_{entirety}}\left|=flatness^{emptiness-2 entirety}\right|_{emptiness} T_{entirety} \right\rvert\,,
\]
whence the required formula follows at once.

garbled_string.question:
4. If \( qzxwvtnp, hjgrksla, \ldots, mnbvcxzr \) are complex numbers such that
\[
\left|qzxwvtnp\right|=\left|hjgrksla\right|=\cdots=\left|mnbvcxzr\right|=poiuytre \neq 0
\]
and if \( { }_{lkjhgfdsa} T_{mikojnuh} \) denotes the sum of all products of these \( lkjhgfdsa \) numbers taken \( mikojnuh \) at a time, prove that
\[
\left|\frac{{ }_{lkjhgfdsa} T_{mikojnuh}}{{ }_{lkjhgfdsa} T_{lkjhgfdsa-mikojnuh}}\right|=poiuytre^{2 mikojnuh-lkjhgfdsa}
\]
whenever the denominator of the left-hand side is different from zero.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. For any non-zero complex number, \( edcrfvtg \), we have \( edcrfvtg \quad 1=\overline{edcrfvtg} /|edcrfvtg|^{2} \) where the bar denotes complex conjugation. If \( yhnujmik \) is a set of \( mikojnuh \) indices selected from \( \{1,2, \ldots, lkjhgfdsa\} \), then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\prod_{qazwsxed \pounds  yhnujmik} plmoknji & =qzxwvtnp\, hjgrksla \cdots mnbvcxzr \prod_{qazwsxed \in yhnujmik} plmoknji^{-1}=qzxwvtnp\, hjgrksla \cdots mnbvcxzr \prod_{qazwsxed \in yhnujmik} \frac{\overline{plmoknji}}{poiuytre^{2}} \\
& =\frac{qzxwvtnp\, hjgrksla \cdots mnbvcxzr}{poiuytre^{2 mikojnuh}} \prod_{qazwsxed \in yhnujmik} \overline{plmoknji}
\end{aligned}
\]

Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
{ }_{lkjhgfdsa} T_{lkjhgfdsa-mikojnuh} & =\sum_{yhnujmik} \prod_{qazwsxed \notin yhnujmik} plmoknji=\frac{qzxwvtnp\, hjgrksla \cdots mnbvcxzr}{poiuytre^{2 mikojnuh}} \sum_{yhnujmik} \prod_{qazwsxed \in yhnujmik} \overline{qzxwvtnp} \\
& =\frac{qzxwvtnp\, hjgrksla \cdots mnbvcxzr}{poiuytre^{2 mikojnuh}} \overline{T}_{mikojnuh} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence
\[
\left.\left|{ }_{lkjhgfdsa} T_{lkjhgfdsa-mikojnuh}\right|=\left.\frac{poiuytre^{lkjhgfdsa}}{poiuytre^{2 mikojnuh}}\right|_{lkjhgfdsa} \overline{T_{mikojnuh}}\left|=poiuytre^{lkjhgfdsa-2 mikojnuh}\right|_{lkjhgfdsa} T_{mikojnuh} \right\rvert\,,
\]
whence the required formula follows at once.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $m\ge 2$ be an integer and let $b_{1},\dots ,b_{m}$ be non-zero complex numbers.  
For $0\le k\le m$ put  
\[
\sigma_{k}\;=\;\sum_{1\le i_{1}<\dots <i_{k}\le m} b_{i_{1}}\dots b_{i_{k}},
\qquad 
\sigma_{0}:=1 .
\]

Fix a real number $\rho>0$ and introduce the anti-linear involution  
\[
\varphi_{\rho}: \Bbb C\setminus\{0\}\longrightarrow\Bbb C\setminus\{0\},
\qquad 
\varphi_{\rho}(z)=\dfrac{\rho^{2}}{\overline z}.
\tag{1}
\]

Define the monic polynomial  
\[
P(z)=\sum_{k=0}^{m} (-1)^{k}\sigma_{k}\,z^{\,m-k}
        =\prod_{j=1}^{m}(z-b_{j}),
\tag{2}
\]
and its $\rho$-conjugate-reciprocal  
\[
P^{\sharp}(z):=z^{\,m}\,\overline{P\!\left(\dfrac{\rho^{2}}{\overline z}\right)} .
\tag{3}
\]

Throughout we shall meet two different coefficient relations
\[
(R_{\rho})\;:\; 
\sigma_{m-k}\neq 0\;\Longrightarrow\;
\left|\frac{\sigma_{k}}{\sigma_{m-k}}\right|
          =\rho^{\,2k-m}
\quad (0\le k\le m),
\tag{4}
\]

\[
(A_{\rho})\;:\;\;
\text{there exists } \lambda\in\Bbb C,\;|\lambda|=1
\text{ such that }\;
\overline{\sigma_{k}}
      =\lambda\,(-1)^{m}\rho^{\,2k-m}\,\sigma_{m-k}
\quad (0\le k\le m).
\tag{5}
\]
(Thus $(A_{\rho})$ refines $(R_{\rho})$ by prescribing the phases.)

Questions  

a)  Prove that $(A_{\rho})$ is equivalent to  
\[
P^{\sharp}(z)=\lambda\,\rho^{\,m}\,P(z)\qquad(\forall z\in\Bbb C);
\tag{6}
\]
polynomials satisfying (6) are called $\rho$-self-reciprocal.

b)  Show that $\rho$-self-reciprocity is equivalent to invariance of the
root multiset under $\varphi_{\rho}$; namely,
\[
\varphi_{\rho}(B)=B,
\qquad B=\{b_{1},\dots ,b_{m}\}.
\tag{7}
\]
Hence the roots can be reordered as  
\[
B=\{w_{1},\varphi_{\rho}(w_{1}),\dots ,w_{s},\varphi_{\rho}(w_{s}),
      u_{1},\dots ,u_{t}\},
\tag{8}
\]
with $|w_{j}|\neq\rho$ and $|u_{i}|=\rho$, $2s+t=m$.  
Conversely every multiset of the form (8) yields a $\rho$-self-reciprocal
polynomial.

c)  Suppose $P$ is $\rho$-self-reciprocal.  
Prove that the following are equivalent:

(i)  $|b_{j}|=\rho$ for every $j$;

(ii) $B$ contains no distinct $\varphi_{\rho}$-pair, that is,
$\varphi_{\rho}(b_{j})=b_{j}$ for all $j$.

In particular, under $(A_{\rho})$ the condition ``no distinct
$\varphi_{\rho}$-pair'' is equivalent to $|b_{j}|=\rho\;(\forall j)$.

d)  Show that $(R_{\rho})$ by itself does \emph{not} imply
$\rho$-self-reciprocity.  More precisely, let $m=2$.

i)   Prove that $(R_{\rho})$ is equivalent to $|b_{1}b_{2}|=\rho^{2}$.

ii)  Prove that $(A_{\rho})$ additionally holds iff, with the unimodular
constant 
\[
\lambda=\frac{\overline{b_{1}b_{2}}}{|b_{1}b_{2}|},
\]
one has  
\[
\overline{b_{1}+b_{2}}=\lambda\,(b_{1}+b_{2}).
\tag{9}
\]

iii) Exhibit concrete numbers
\[
b_{1}=2i,\qquad b_{2}=1,\qquad \rho=\sqrt 2,
\]
for which $(R_{\rho})$ is valid while (9) fails, thereby giving a
quadratic example with $(R_{\rho})$ but \emph{not} $(A_{\rho})$.



----------------------------------------------------------------------
kernel_variant.solution:
We keep the notation of the statement throughout.

0.  Coefficients of $P^{\sharp}$.  
Expanding (3) with (2) gives  
\[
\begin{aligned}
P^{\sharp}(z)
      &=z^{\,m}\sum_{k=0}^{m}(-1)^{k}\,
           \overline{\sigma_{k}}\,
           \left(\dfrac{\rho^{2}}{\overline z}\right)^{m-k}
      =\sum_{k=0}^{m}(-1)^{k}\rho^{\,2(m-k)}
           \overline{\sigma_{k}}\,
           z^{\,k}.
\end{aligned}
\tag{10}
\]

Hence, for $0\le k\le m$,  
\[
p_{k}=(-1)^{\,m-k}\sigma_{m-k}= \operatorname{coeff}_{z^{\,k}}P,
\qquad
q_{k}=(-1)^{k}\rho^{\,2(m-k)}
      \overline{\sigma_{k}}
      =\operatorname{coeff}_{z^{\,k}}P^{\sharp}.
\tag{11}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Part (a) $(A_{\rho})\Longleftrightarrow$ (6)
--------------------------------------------------------------------

(i)  $(A_{\rho})\Rightarrow$ (6).  
Insert (5) into $q_{k}$:
\[
\begin{aligned}
q_{k}
   &=(-1)^{k}\rho^{\,2(m-k)}
       \lambda\,(-1)^{m}\rho^{\,2k-m}\,\sigma_{m-k}  \\
   &=\lambda\,(-1)^{m-k}\rho^{\,m}\sigma_{m-k}
   =\lambda\,\rho^{\,m}\,p_{k}\qquad(0\le k\le m).
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus $P^{\sharp}=\lambda\rho^{m}P$.

(ii)  (6)$\Rightarrow(A_{\rho})$.  
Assume $P^{\sharp}=cP$ with $c\in\Bbb C^{\times}$.  
Comparing the coefficients of $z^{m}$ in $P^{\sharp}$ and $P$ (see (2),
(10)) gives  
\[
c=(-1)^{m}\overline{\sigma_{m}},\qquad |c|=|\sigma_{m}|.
\tag{12}
\]
Comparing the constant terms yields  
\[
c=\frac{\rho^{2m}}{(-1)^{m}\sigma_{m}},\qquad
|c|=\frac{\rho^{2m}}{|\sigma_{m}|}.
\tag{13}
\]
Equating the moduli in (12) and (13) gives $|\sigma_{m}|^{2}=\rho^{2m}$,
hence $|\sigma_{m}|=\rho^{m}$ and $|c|=\rho^{m}$.
Write $c=\lambda\rho^{m}$ with $|\lambda|=1$.  
Because $P^{\sharp}=cP$ we have $q_{k}=c\,p_{k}$ for \emph{every}
$k=0,\dots ,m$.  Substituting the explicit forms (11) gives
\[
(-1)^{k}\rho^{\,2(m-k)}\overline{\sigma_{k}}
      =\lambda\rho^{m}(-1)^{\,m-k}\sigma_{m-k},
\]
which rearranges exactly to (5) for all $k$.  
Thus (6) implies $(A_{\rho})$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Part (b) $\rho$-self-reciprocity $\Longleftrightarrow$
          $\varphi_{\rho}$-invariance
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Suppose $P$ satisfies (6).  If $b\in B$ then
\[
0=P(b)=\lambda^{-1}\rho^{-m}P^{\sharp}(b)
      =\lambda^{-1}\rho^{-m}b^{\,m}\,
        \overline{P\!\bigl(\varphi_{\rho}(b)\bigr)} ,
\]
so $P\!\bigl(\varphi_{\rho}(b)\bigr)=0$ and $\varphi_{\rho}(b)\in B$.
Counting multiplicities proves (7).

Conversely, assume (7).  
Then $P$ and $P^{\sharp}$ share the same zeros, hence
$P^{\sharp}=cP$ for some $c\neq 0$.
Using the argument of part (a) we already know $|c|=\rho^{m}$, so
$c=\lambda\rho^{m}$ with $|\lambda|=1$ and (6) holds.

Root decomposition.  
Because $\varphi_{\rho}$ is an involution, each orbit in $B$ is either a
two-cycle $\{w,\varphi_{\rho}(w)\}$ with $|w|\neq\rho$ or a fixed point
$u$ with $|u|=\rho$.  Re-ordering the roots yields (8).  
Conversely any multiset of the form (8) is $\varphi_{\rho}$-stable, so
its monic polynomial is $\rho$-self-reciprocal by the first part of (b).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Part (c) All roots on the circle
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Assume $P$ is $\rho$-self-reciprocal.  

(i)$\Rightarrow$(ii).  
If $|b_{j}|=\rho$ then
$\varphi_{\rho}(b_{j})=\rho^{2}/\overline{b_{j}}=b_{j}$, so $B$ contains
no distinct $\varphi_{\rho}$-pair.

(ii)$\Rightarrow$(i).  
Conversely, if every $\varphi_{\rho}$-orbit is trivial then (8) has
$s=0$, hence every root is fixed by $\varphi_{\rho}$ and consequently
has modulus $\rho$.  

Since $\rho$-self-reciprocity is equivalent to $(A_{\rho})$ by (a), the
last sentence follows immediately.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Part (d) $(R_{\rho})\nRightarrow(A_{\rho})$
          in the quadratic case
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Let $m=2$.  Then $\sigma_{1}=b_{1}+b_{2}$ and $\sigma_{2}=b_{1}b_{2}$.

(i)  From $k=0$ or $k=2$ in (4) we obtain  
\[
|\sigma_{2}|=\rho^{2}\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
|b_{1}b_{2}|=\rho^{2},
\]
while the $k=1$ condition is automatically satisfied.
Thus $(R_{\rho})\iff |b_{1}b_{2}|=\rho^{2}$.

(ii)  Assume $(R_{\rho})$.  
Set 
\[
\lambda=\frac{\overline{\sigma_{2}}}{|\sigma_{2}|}\quad(|\lambda|=1).
\]
Condition (5) for $k=2$ is then automatically fulfilled, and for $k=0$
is trivial.  The remaining case $k=1$ yields precisely (9).  
Hence $(A_{\rho})$ holds iff (9) is satisfied.

(iii)  Take  
\[
b_{1}=2i,\qquad b_{2}=1,\qquad \rho=\sqrt 2 .
\]
Then $\sigma_{2}=2i$ so $|\sigma_{2}|=2=\rho^{2}$, i.e.\ $(R_{\rho})$
holds.  With 
\[
\lambda=\frac{\overline{\sigma_{2}}}{|\sigma_{2}|}
        =\frac{-2i}{2}=-i
\]
we have  
\[
\overline{b_{1}+b_{2}}=1-2i,
\qquad
\lambda\,(b_{1}+b_{2})=-i(1+2i)=2-i,
\]
which are different; hence (9) fails and $(A_{\rho})$ is not satisfied.
This exhibits a quadratic polynomial for which $(R_{\rho})$ holds
whereas $(A_{\rho})$ (and therefore $\rho$-self-reciprocity) fails.

\hfill$\square$


----------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Extra directions: the original problem only asks for the forward implication “equal moduli ⇒ ratio of symmetric sums”.  The enhanced variant additionally demands  
   • the converse (deducing equal moduli from the ratios),  
   • a functional identity between a polynomial and its reciprocal, and  
   • a spectral characterisation of the location of its zeros.  
   Each new claim introduces a fresh layer of algebraic and analytic reasoning.

2. Technical depth: proving the converse requires comparing two polynomials whose coefficients match in modulus but not necessarily in argument, and showing that they must possess the same multiset of zeros.  This employs
   • reversed–conjugate (reciprocal) polynomials,  
   • identification of root multisets via coefficient data, and  
   • a multiset argument to force individual modulus equality.

3. Interacting concepts: the solution blends classical symmetric-function manipulations with complex conjugation, polynomial reciprocity, and root-set arguments.  The final equivalence in part (c) brings in elementary potential theory (location of zeros on a circle) tying algebraic constraints on coefficients to geometric constraints on roots.

4. Increased workload: besides re-proving the original statement, the solver must create and analyse a new polynomial (the reciprocal), handle unimodular ambiguities, and carry out a non-trivial multiset comparison—all steps absent from the original exercise.

These additions push the problem well beyond routine symmetric-sum manipulation, making it substantially harder than both the original statement and its immediate kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $m\ge 2$ be an integer and let $b_{1},\dots ,b_{m}$ be non-zero complex numbers.  
For $0\le k\le m$ put  
\[
\sigma_{k}\;=\;\sum_{1\le i_{1}<\dots <i_{k}\le m} b_{i_{1}}\dots b_{i_{k}},
\qquad 
\sigma_{0}:=1 .
\]

Fix a real number $\rho>0$ and introduce the anti-linear involution  
\[
\varphi_{\rho}: \Bbb C\setminus\{0\}\longrightarrow\Bbb C\setminus\{0\},
\qquad 
\varphi_{\rho}(z)=\dfrac{\rho^{2}}{\overline z}.
\tag{1}
\]

Define the monic polynomial  
\[
P(z)=\sum_{k=0}^{m} (-1)^{k}\sigma_{k}\,z^{\,m-k}
        =\prod_{j=1}^{m}(z-b_{j}),
\tag{2}
\]
and its $\rho$-conjugate-reciprocal  
\[
P^{\sharp}(z):=z^{\,m}\,\overline{P\!\left(\dfrac{\rho^{2}}{\overline z}\right)} .
\tag{3}
\]

Throughout we shall meet two different coefficient relations
\[
(R_{\rho})\;:\; 
\sigma_{m-k}\neq 0\;\Longrightarrow\;
\left|\frac{\sigma_{k}}{\sigma_{m-k}}\right|
          =\rho^{\,2k-m}
\quad (0\le k\le m),
\tag{4}
\]

\[
(A_{\rho})\;:\;\;
\text{there exists } \lambda\in\Bbb C,\;|\lambda|=1
\text{ such that }\;
\overline{\sigma_{k}}
      =\lambda\,(-1)^{m}\rho^{\,2k-m}\,\sigma_{m-k}
\quad (0\le k\le m).
\tag{5}
\]
(Thus $(A_{\rho})$ refines $(R_{\rho})$ by prescribing the phases.)

Questions  

a)  Prove that $(A_{\rho})$ is equivalent to  
\[
P^{\sharp}(z)=\lambda\,\rho^{\,m}\,P(z)\qquad(\forall z\in\Bbb C);
\tag{6}
\]
polynomials satisfying (6) are called $\rho$-self-reciprocal.

b)  Show that $\rho$-self-reciprocity is equivalent to invariance of the
root multiset under $\varphi_{\rho}$; namely,
\[
\varphi_{\rho}(B)=B,
\qquad B=\{b_{1},\dots ,b_{m}\}.
\tag{7}
\]
Hence the roots can be reordered as  
\[
B=\{w_{1},\varphi_{\rho}(w_{1}),\dots ,w_{s},\varphi_{\rho}(w_{s}),
      u_{1},\dots ,u_{t}\},
\tag{8}
\]
with $|w_{j}|\neq\rho$ and $|u_{i}|=\rho$, $2s+t=m$.  
Conversely every multiset of the form (8) yields a $\rho$-self-reciprocal
polynomial.

c)  Suppose $P$ is $\rho$-self-reciprocal.  
Prove that the following are equivalent:

(i)  $|b_{j}|=\rho$ for every $j$;

(ii) $B$ contains no distinct $\varphi_{\rho}$-pair, that is,
$\varphi_{\rho}(b_{j})=b_{j}$ for all $j$.

In particular, under $(A_{\rho})$ the condition ``no distinct
$\varphi_{\rho}$-pair'' is equivalent to $|b_{j}|=\rho\;(\forall j)$.

d)  Show that $(R_{\rho})$ by itself does \emph{not} imply
$\rho$-self-reciprocity.  More precisely, let $m=2$.

i)   Prove that $(R_{\rho})$ is equivalent to $|b_{1}b_{2}|=\rho^{2}$.

ii)  Prove that $(A_{\rho})$ additionally holds iff, with the unimodular
constant 
\[
\lambda=\frac{\overline{b_{1}b_{2}}}{|b_{1}b_{2}|},
\]
one has  
\[
\overline{b_{1}+b_{2}}=\lambda\,(b_{1}+b_{2}).
\tag{9}
\]

iii) Exhibit concrete numbers
\[
b_{1}=2i,\qquad b_{2}=1,\qquad \rho=\sqrt 2,
\]
for which $(R_{\rho})$ is valid while (9) fails, thereby giving a
quadratic example with $(R_{\rho})$ but \emph{not} $(A_{\rho})$.



----------------------------------------------------------------------
original_kernel_variant.solution:
We keep the notation of the statement throughout.

0.  Coefficients of $P^{\sharp}$.  
Expanding (3) with (2) gives  
\[
\begin{aligned}
P^{\sharp}(z)
      &=z^{\,m}\sum_{k=0}^{m}(-1)^{k}\,
           \overline{\sigma_{k}}\,
           \left(\dfrac{\rho^{2}}{\overline z}\right)^{m-k}
      =\sum_{k=0}^{m}(-1)^{k}\rho^{\,2(m-k)}
           \overline{\sigma_{k}}\,
           z^{\,k}.
\end{aligned}
\tag{10}
\]

Hence, for $0\le k\le m$,  
\[
p_{k}=(-1)^{\,m-k}\sigma_{m-k}= \operatorname{coeff}_{z^{\,k}}P,
\qquad
q_{k}=(-1)^{k}\rho^{\,2(m-k)}
      \overline{\sigma_{k}}
      =\operatorname{coeff}_{z^{\,k}}P^{\sharp}.
\tag{11}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Part (a) $(A_{\rho})\Longleftrightarrow$ (6)
--------------------------------------------------------------------

(i)  $(A_{\rho})\Rightarrow$ (6).  
Insert (5) into $q_{k}$:
\[
\begin{aligned}
q_{k}
   &=(-1)^{k}\rho^{\,2(m-k)}
       \lambda\,(-1)^{m}\rho^{\,2k-m}\,\sigma_{m-k}  \\
   &=\lambda\,(-1)^{m-k}\rho^{\,m}\sigma_{m-k}
   =\lambda\,\rho^{\,m}\,p_{k}\qquad(0\le k\le m).
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus $P^{\sharp}=\lambda\rho^{m}P$.

(ii)  (6)$\Rightarrow(A_{\rho})$.  
Assume $P^{\sharp}=cP$ with $c\in\Bbb C^{\times}$.  
Comparing the coefficients of $z^{m}$ in $P^{\sharp}$ and $P$ (see (2),
(10)) gives  
\[
c=(-1)^{m}\overline{\sigma_{m}},\qquad |c|=|\sigma_{m}|.
\tag{12}
\]
Comparing the constant terms yields  
\[
c=\frac{\rho^{2m}}{(-1)^{m}\sigma_{m}},\qquad
|c|=\frac{\rho^{2m}}{|\sigma_{m}|}.
\tag{13}
\]
Equating the moduli in (12) and (13) gives $|\sigma_{m}|^{2}=\rho^{2m}$,
hence $|\sigma_{m}|=\rho^{m}$ and $|c|=\rho^{m}$.
Write $c=\lambda\rho^{m}$ with $|\lambda|=1$.  
Because $P^{\sharp}=cP$ we have $q_{k}=c\,p_{k}$ for \emph{every}
$k=0,\dots ,m$.  Substituting the explicit forms (11) gives
\[
(-1)^{k}\rho^{\,2(m-k)}\overline{\sigma_{k}}
      =\lambda\rho^{m}(-1)^{\,m-k}\sigma_{m-k},
\]
which rearranges exactly to (5) for all $k$.  
Thus (6) implies $(A_{\rho})$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Part (b) $\rho$-self-reciprocity $\Longleftrightarrow$
          $\varphi_{\rho}$-invariance
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Suppose $P$ satisfies (6).  If $b\in B$ then
\[
0=P(b)=\lambda^{-1}\rho^{-m}P^{\sharp}(b)
      =\lambda^{-1}\rho^{-m}b^{\,m}\,
        \overline{P\!\bigl(\varphi_{\rho}(b)\bigr)} ,
\]
so $P\!\bigl(\varphi_{\rho}(b)\bigr)=0$ and $\varphi_{\rho}(b)\in B$.
Counting multiplicities proves (7).

Conversely, assume (7).  
Then $P$ and $P^{\sharp}$ share the same zeros, hence
$P^{\sharp}=cP$ for some $c\neq 0$.
Using the argument of part (a) we already know $|c|=\rho^{m}$, so
$c=\lambda\rho^{m}$ with $|\lambda|=1$ and (6) holds.

Root decomposition.  
Because $\varphi_{\rho}$ is an involution, each orbit in $B$ is either a
two-cycle $\{w,\varphi_{\rho}(w)\}$ with $|w|\neq\rho$ or a fixed point
$u$ with $|u|=\rho$.  Re-ordering the roots yields (8).  
Conversely any multiset of the form (8) is $\varphi_{\rho}$-stable, so
its monic polynomial is $\rho$-self-reciprocal by the first part of (b).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Part (c) All roots on the circle
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Assume $P$ is $\rho$-self-reciprocal.  

(i)$\Rightarrow$(ii).  
If $|b_{j}|=\rho$ then
$\varphi_{\rho}(b_{j})=\rho^{2}/\overline{b_{j}}=b_{j}$, so $B$ contains
no distinct $\varphi_{\rho}$-pair.

(ii)$\Rightarrow$(i).  
Conversely, if every $\varphi_{\rho}$-orbit is trivial then (8) has
$s=0$, hence every root is fixed by $\varphi_{\rho}$ and consequently
has modulus $\rho$.  

Since $\rho$-self-reciprocity is equivalent to $(A_{\rho})$ by (a), the
last sentence follows immediately.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Part (d) $(R_{\rho})\nRightarrow(A_{\rho})$
          in the quadratic case
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Let $m=2$.  Then $\sigma_{1}=b_{1}+b_{2}$ and $\sigma_{2}=b_{1}b_{2}$.

(i)  From $k=0$ or $k=2$ in (4) we obtain  
\[
|\sigma_{2}|=\rho^{2}\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
|b_{1}b_{2}|=\rho^{2},
\]
while the $k=1$ condition is automatically satisfied.
Thus $(R_{\rho})\iff |b_{1}b_{2}|=\rho^{2}$.

(ii)  Assume $(R_{\rho})$.  
Set 
\[
\lambda=\frac{\overline{\sigma_{2}}}{|\sigma_{2}|}\quad(|\lambda|=1).
\]
Condition (5) for $k=2$ is then automatically fulfilled, and for $k=0$
is trivial.  The remaining case $k=1$ yields precisely (9).  
Hence $(A_{\rho})$ holds iff (9) is satisfied.

(iii)  Take  
\[
b_{1}=2i,\qquad b_{2}=1,\qquad \rho=\sqrt 2 .
\]
Then $\sigma_{2}=2i$ so $|\sigma_{2}|=2=\rho^{2}$, i.e.\ $(R_{\rho})$
holds.  With 
\[
\lambda=\frac{\overline{\sigma_{2}}}{|\sigma_{2}|}
        =\frac{-2i}{2}=-i
\]
we have  
\[
\overline{b_{1}+b_{2}}=1-2i,
\qquad
\lambda\,(b_{1}+b_{2})=-i(1+2i)=2-i,
\]
which are different; hence (9) fails and $(A_{\rho})$ is not satisfied.
This exhibits a quadratic polynomial for which $(R_{\rho})$ holds
whereas $(A_{\rho})$ (and therefore $\rho$-self-reciprocity) fails.

\hfill$\square$


----------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Extra directions: the original problem only asks for the forward implication “equal moduli ⇒ ratio of symmetric sums”.  The enhanced variant additionally demands  
   • the converse (deducing equal moduli from the ratios),  
   • a functional identity between a polynomial and its reciprocal, and  
   • a spectral characterisation of the location of its zeros.  
   Each new claim introduces a fresh layer of algebraic and analytic reasoning.

2. Technical depth: proving the converse requires comparing two polynomials whose coefficients match in modulus but not necessarily in argument, and showing that they must possess the same multiset of zeros.  This employs
   • reversed–conjugate (reciprocal) polynomials,  
   • identification of root multisets via coefficient data, and  
   • a multiset argument to force individual modulus equality.

3. Interacting concepts: the solution blends classical symmetric-function manipulations with complex conjugation, polynomial reciprocity, and root-set arguments.  The final equivalence in part (c) brings in elementary potential theory (location of zeros on a circle) tying algebraic constraints on coefficients to geometric constraints on roots.

4. Increased workload: besides re-proving the original statement, the solver must create and analyse a new polynomial (the reciprocal), handle unimodular ambiguities, and carry out a non-trivial multiset comparison—all steps absent from the original exercise.

These additions push the problem well beyond routine symmetric-sum manipulation, making it substantially harder than both the original statement and its immediate kernel variant.
